file_name: Rey-Barroso et al. - 2019 - Morphological study of skin cancer lesions through a 3D scanner based on fringe projection and machi.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used supervised machine learning algorithms, specifically decision trees (Bagged Trees) and nearest neighbors (Weighted k-Nearest Neighbor), implemented via the Matlab R2015a Classification Learner application, to classify skin lesions (melanomas vs. nevi) based on 3D morphological parameters obtained from a fringe projection scanner. The study reported classification performance metrics including sensitivity (80.0%) and specificity (76.7%).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel 3D scanner based on fringe projection and the application of supervised machine learning algorithms to classify skin lesions. It reports primary data from 608 measured samples, statistical analysis (p<0.001), and classification performance metrics (80.0% sensitivity, 76.7% specificity), which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Morphological study of skin cancer lesions through a 3D scanner based on fringe projection and machine learning
brief_summary: This study developed a novel handheld 3D scanner based on fringe projection to non-invasively measure morphological parameters of skin lesions with micrometric precision. The researchers measured 608 samples from patients at hospitals in Spain and Italy, focusing on distinguishing between melanomas and benign nevi. Significant morphological differences were found between melanomas and nevi (p<0.001) regarding area, volume, and perimeter. Supervised machine learning algorithms, specifically decision trees and weighted k-nearest neighbors, were applied to classify the lesions using these 3D parameters. The best classification performance was achieved using a holdout validation scheme with a Weighted KNN model, resulting in 80.0% sensitivity and 76.7% specificity. These results demonstrate the potential of 3D morphological analysis combined with machine learning to improve the non-invasive diagnosis of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: 60 lesions included in the final classification dataset; 60 out of 654 total assessed lesions; significant morphological differences found compared to nevi (p<0.001).
81 benign nevi initially collected; 60 randomly chosen for the final classification dataset to balance with melanomas; significant morphological differences found compared to melanomas (p<0.001).
18 lesions measured; excluded from statistical analysis and machine learning classification due to low sample size; compared against melanomas and nevi for morphological parameters.
18 lesions (9% of measured); excluded from statistical analysis and machine learning due to low sample size and grouping as a heterogeneous category.
11 lesions (6% of measured); excluded from statistical analysis and machine learning due to low sample size.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Benign Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The study used supervised machine learning algorithms (Bagged Trees and Weighted k-Nearest Neighbor) to classify skin lesions into two discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma and benign nevus. The model was trained on 3D morphological parameters (area, volume, perimeter) extracted from a 3D scanner. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity (80.0%) and specificity (76.7%) via Holdout Validation.

datasets_used.name: Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelona and Università di Modena e Reggio Emilia Cohort
datasets_used.description: A clinical cohort of skin lesions measured using a novel handheld 3D scanner based on fringe projection. The study assessed 654 lesions from Caucasian individuals at two hospitals in Spain and Italy. After quality control (excluding artifacts, out-of-FOV, and hair issues), 194 lesions were analyzed morphologically. For the machine learning classification task, a balanced subset of 120 lesions was used.

use.training: Used to train supervised machine learning models (Bagged Trees, Weighted KNN) using 3D morphological parameters (area, volume, perimeter) for the classification of Melanoma vs. Benign Nevus. The dataset was balanced by randomly selecting 60 nevi from the 81 available.
use.validation: Used for validation via 'Holdout Validation' (50% training, 50% validation) and 'Cross Validation' (k=2) schemes to evaluate classification performance (sensitivity and specificity).
use.other: The full cohort of 194 lesions was used for statistical analysis of morphological differences (ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis tests) among lesion types. Basal Cell Carcinoma, Non-nevi benign lesions, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma were excluded from the machine learning classification due to low sample sizes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the normal distribution of all morphological variables.
To compare data among groups of skin lesions for parametric variables.
To compare data among groups of skin lesions for non-parametric variables.
To compare outcome measures between pairs of groups for normally distributed data.
To compare outcome measures between pairs of groups for non-normally distributed data.
To validate the classification model by dividing data into two folds (50% training, 50% validation) to assess sensitivity, specificity, and error rate.
To validate the classification model by dividing data into two subfolds, training on out-of-fold observations, and calculating average test error to assess repeatability.
To test an algorithm where all data is used for both training and validating.
To determine statistical significance for comparisons of morphological parameters.

hypothesis_testing.test: Kruskal-Wallis (KW) test for group comparisons; Mann-Whitney U (MWU) test for pairwise comparisons; Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for normality assessment.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no significant differences in the morphological parameters (area, volume, perimeter, etc.) among the different types of skin lesions (melanomas, nevi, basal cell carcinomas).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant differences in the morphological parameters among the different types of skin lesions.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined by p-values under 0.05. The KW test reported significant differences for area, volume, perimeter, normalized area, and normalized volume among lesion types (p < 0.001). The MWU test confirmed significant differences between melanomas and nevi (p < 0.001) for all parameters, while differences between melanomas/nevi and basal cell carcinomas were generally not significant, except for area between BCC and MM (p = 0.034).

Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelon.dataset_description: Balanced subset of 120 lesions (60 melanomas, 60 nevi) used for machine learning classification. Full cohort of 194 lesions used for statistical morphological analysis.

morphological_differences.melanoma_vs_nevi: Significant differences found for area, volume, perimeter, normalized area, and normalized volume (p < 0.001).
morphological_differences.melanoma_vs_bcc: No significant differences except for area (p = 0.034).

Cross Validation.notes: Maximum variations of 5.00% for sensitivity and specificity and 0.05% of error rate across different random sets of nevi.

Holdout Validation.notes: Best results obtained. Variations of sensitivity, specificity and error rate were 10.00%, 13.40% and 11.65%, respectively. K=10 neighbors used.

No Validation.model: Not specified (all data used for training and validating)
No Validation.notes: Data set was classified with respect to the data set itself.

Hospital Clínic i Provincial de Barcelon.notes: Profile and amplitude parameters calculated following ISO 25178 did not provide significant differences (p > 0.05). Basal cell carcinomas, non-nevi benign lesions, seborrheic keratosis, and squamous cell carcinomas were excluded from machine learning classification due to low sample sizes.

architecture_used.main_model: Supervised machine learning algorithms implemented via Matlab R2015a Classification Learner application, specifically Bagged Trees (decision trees) and Weighted k-Nearest Neighbor (KNN).
architecture_used.input_size: 3D morphological parameters (area, volume, perimeter, Ap, Vp) extracted from 3D scanner data.

training_details.validation_scheme: Holdout Validation (50% training, 50% validation) and Cross Validation (k=2)

preprocessing_techniques: Leveling of the scanned surface to remove general slope caused by small tilts
Zooming to select the Region of Interest (ROI) containing the lesion and surrounding healthy skin
Application of a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) filter to exclude frequencies corresponding to motion artifacts
Segmentation of lesions from healthy skin on the color image
Conversion of 3D surface into 2D profiles (horizontal and vertical axial cuts)
conclusions: Significant morphological differences were found between melanomas and benign nevi regarding area, volume, and perimeter (p<0.001), while profile and amplitude parameters based on ISO 25178 did not show significant differences.
The 3D scanner based on fringe projection successfully measured morphological parameters with micrometric precision, enabling the non-invasive assessment of skin lesions.
Supervised machine learning algorithms, specifically Weighted k-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) and Bagged Trees, were effective in classifying melanomas versus nevi using 3D morphological parameters.
The best classification performance was achieved using a Holdout Validation scheme with a Weighted KNN model, resulting in 80.0% sensitivity and 76.7% specificity.

Discriminating basal cell carcinomas from melanomas and nevi proved complex, as significant differences were found only for area between basal cell carcinomas and melanomas (p=0.034), with no significant differences between basal cell carcinomas and nevi.
The study demonstrates the potential of combining 3D morphological analysis with machine learning to improve the non-invasive diagnosis of skin cancer and reduce unnecessary biopsies.
important_remarks: The study population is limited to Caucasian individuals from two specific hospitals in Spain and Italy, which may limit the generalizability of the morphological findings to other ethnic groups with different skin pigmentation or texture characteristics.

A significant portion of the initial cohort (68% of measured lesions) was excluded from analysis due to artifacts (micro-movements), lesions falling outside the field of view, or hair interference, indicating potential selection bias and practical deployment challenges regarding patient cooperation and lesion accessibility.

The machine learning classification was restricted to a balanced subset of 120 lesions (60 melanomas, 60 nevi); other malignancies such as Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Seborrheic Keratosis were excluded from the ML task due to low sample sizes, limiting the model's utility for differential diagnosis of non-melanoma skin cancers.

Discrimination between Basal Cell Carcinomas and Melanomas/Nevis proved difficult, with no significant morphological differences found for most parameters (only area showed significance between BCC and MM), suggesting that 3D morphological parameters alone may be insufficient for distinguishing BCC from other lesions.

The 'No Validation' scheme yielded 100% sensitivity and specificity, highlighting the risk of data leakage if training and testing data are not strictly separated; the authors correctly identified 'Holdout Validation' and 'Cross Validation' as more reliable metrics.

Profile and amplitude parameters based on ISO 25178 standards did not show significant differences between lesion types (p > 0.05), indicating that standard surface roughness metrics may not be as discriminative as basic geometric parameters (area, volume, perimeter) for this specific application.

The study reports high variability in performance metrics across different random subsets of nevi (e.g., up to 13.40% variation in specificity for Holdout Validation), suggesting that the model's performance may be sensitive to the specific composition of the benign nevus training data.
